ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a dedicated spring chicken (poussin) raw skinless entry; closest defensible match is chicken meat (raw) FOODON_03411433. Spring chicken is a young broiler-type bird; nutritional profile is consistent with raw skinless chicken.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00GJ covers raw chicken meat broadly; no dedicated spring chicken or poussin sub-code exists in the public FoodEx2 catalogue. Close match accepted.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept specifically for spring chicken raw without skin identified in public SNOMED CT release; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 small whole spring chicken (approx. 400 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw spring chicken without skin is approximately 400 g, representing roughly one whole small bird prepared for cooking.

nutrient_source.title: Spring chicken, raw, without skin — Singapore HPB Foodo Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Foodo record F11010042 is an exact match for spring chicken raw without skin. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are sourced directly from this record. Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011 (chicken broilers or fryers, meat only, raw) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry; documented in notes.

gi_source.title: N/A — Glycemic index not applicable for negligible-carbohydrate protein food
gi_source.source_match_notes: Spring chicken raw without skin contains 0 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates. No GI value is assigned. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 by definition. glycemic_index field is set to null (not 0) per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium (77 mg/100 g) with favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (229 mg potassium/100 g), consistent with DASH diet principles
hypertension.guidance: Spring chicken raw without skin is a hypertension-suitable lean protein; prepare by steaming, grilling, or poaching without added salt or high-sodium condiments such as soy sauce or oyster sauce to preserve its low-sodium advantage.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero carbohydrate and zero sugar; high protein (21.39 g/100 g) with no direct glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: Spring chicken raw without skin is an excellent protein source for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its zero carbohydrate content and high protein density, which slows gastric emptying; avoid sweet marinades or glazes that add sugar, and pair with low-GI vegetables rather than refined starches.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low total fat (3.08 g/100 g) and low saturated fat (~0.87 g/100 g); cholesterol at 70 mg/100 g is moderate and consistent with lean poultry
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Spring chicken raw without skin is a heart-healthy lean protein choice with low saturated fat; cooking methods such as steaming or grilling without added oils or high-sodium sauces are recommended to maintain its cardiovascular benefit, and portion size should be managed to keep dietary cholesterol within recommended limits.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lean white meat with low saturated fat (~0.87 g/100 g), well below the 5 g RA threshold; however, no positive anti-inflammatory factors present (no omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols, or whole-food fibre above 3 g)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Spring chicken raw without skin is nutritionally neutral for rheumatoid arthritis — its low saturated fat does not worsen inflammation, but it lacks anti-inflammatory factors; avoid deep-frying (which generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation) and pair with anti-inflammatory herbs such as turmeric, ginger, or garlic to enhance benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spring chicken, raw, without skin (HPB F11010042) is a whole young chicken — typically a bird slaughtered at 4–6 weeks of age and weighing 400–600 g — with the skin fully removed before cooking. The raw flesh is pale pink, fine-grained, and notably tender compared to mature broiler chicken, with a delicate, mild flavour that absorbs marinades and aromatics readily. As a raw ingredient, it is used across a wide range of cuisines including Singaporean, Chinese, Western, and Southeast Asian cooking, commonly prepared by steaming, roasting, grilling, or braising. Nutritionally, spring chicken without skin is a high-protein, low-fat food: per 100 g it provides 119 kcal, 21.39 g protein, 3.08 g total fat (approximately 0.87 g saturated fat), and 0 g carbohydrate. Sodium is low at 77 mg/100 g and potassium is 229 mg/100 g, yielding a favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio aligned with DASH diet principles. Cholesterol is 70 mg/100 g, consistent with lean poultry. The absence of carbohydrate and sugar means glycemic index is not applicable. This food is suitable for Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease management when prepared without high-sodium condiments or added sugars. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free. Data sourced from Singapore HPB Foodo database, record F11010042.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from Singapore HPB Foodo database record F11010042, which is an exact match for spring chicken raw without skin. Confidence is 0.95 per the source record. Saturated fat and fatty acid breakdown estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011 (chicken broilers or fryers, meat only, raw) as a close analogue, which slightly reduces certainty for those specific fields but does not affect overall high confidence for the primary macros.
notes: 1. ZERO-MACRO JUSTIFICATION: carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as spring chicken is a pure animal protein food that inherently contains no carbohydrates. Sugar_g is naturally zero as this food contains no added or intrinsic sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero as animal muscle tissue contains no dietary fibre. These are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo F11010042 and consistent with USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as these micronutrient values are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo F11010042 record and were not available in a directly matching approved source entry at time of processing. 3. SATURATED FAT ESTIMATION: saturated_fat_g (0.87 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (1.15 g), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.67 g), and trans_fat_g (0.02 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011 (chicken broilers or fryers, meat only, raw) as HPB Foodo does not separately report fatty acid fractions for this record. Total fat cross-check: 0.87 + 1.15 + 0.67 + 0.02 = 2.71 g vs HPB-reported 3.08 g total fat; minor rounding difference is within expected analytical variance for fatty acid sub-fractions. 4. GI: Glycemic index is not applicable as this food contains negligible carbohydrates (0 g/100 g). glycemic_index set to null (not numeric zero) per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate protein foods. gi_category set to not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving set to 0.0 by definition. 5. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 and FoodEx2 A00GJ are closest_parent matches; no dedicated spring chicken or poussin raw skinless code exists in public releases of either system as of 2026-04-16. 6. MULTILINGUAL: Spring chicken raw without skin is a generic international ingredient, not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names apply under G6 multilingual rules. Local name fields are null by design. 7. Q4 FIX (iteration 3): Previous iteration validator flagged glycemic_index must be numeric. Per the critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods, glycemic_index must be null (not 0 and not a numeric value), and gi_category must be not_applicable. The validator feedback appears to have expected a numeric value; however, the controlling rule in the execution prompt explicitly states: set glycemic_index to null for foods with negligible carbohydrates, and that setting glycemic_index=0 will cause a q4 gate failure. null is the correct and required value. This record conforms to the critical rule as stated.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name. Food is a generic international ingredient, not a cuisine-specific dish, so no mandatory local-language identity applies under G6. Aliases cover alternate English forms including poussin and baby chicken.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal (119.0), protein_g (21.39), fat_g (3.08), and carbohydrate_g (0.0) are all present, numeric, and sourced from HPB Foodo F11010042 (exact match). Zero carbohydrate is justified in notes as naturally zero for an animal protein food.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g (0.0 — naturally zero, animal tissue), sodium_mg (77.0), saturated_fat_g (0.87 — estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011, documented in notes), and sugar_g (0.0 — naturally zero) are all present as numeric values and plausible.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index is set to null per the mandatory critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods (0 g carbohydrate/100 g). Setting glycemic_index=0 is explicitly prohibited by the execution rules and would cause a q4 gate failure. null is the required and correct value. gi_category is not_applicable. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. gi_source is present and documents the not-applicable determination with reference to University of Sydney GI Database. All four q4 sub-fields are present and correctly populated.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn FOODON_03411433 and FoodEx2 A00GJ are defensible closest_parent matches. SNOMED CT is null with honest gap note. No codes invented. Gap notes explain why exact codes do not exist for spring chicken specifically.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (approximately 1,100 characters). It covers what the food is, preparation methods, taste and texture, cultural context, clinical relevance, and key nutrients. common_user_queries and meal_context are populated.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (low concern, human_condition_name correctly set to Type 2 Diabetes, machine key type2_diabetes), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. Rheumatoid arthritis included as additional condition.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 6 entries covering canonical name, skinless variant, poussin (international synonym), baby chicken, young chicken, and whole spring chicken skinless raw. aliases_by_language english array mirrors all 6 entries verbatim. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language.english appear verbatim in flat aliases array. Food is a generic ingredient so no mandatory multilingual aliases apply under G6.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Corrected ontology match types from 'close' to 'closest_parent' to resolve G5 concern.

Iteration 3: Confirmed glycemic_index remains null per critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate foods. Added explicit q4 notes clarifying that null is the required value per execution prompt rules, not a numeric value. Validator feedback in iteration 2 flagged glycemic_index must be numeric; however, the controlling execution prompt rule explicitly mandates null for zero-carbohydrate foods and prohibits setting glycemic_index=0. null is retained as the correct and compliant value. Added note 7 to notes field documenting this resolution.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: Spring chicken raw without skin (HPB F11010042) passes all six hard gates and scores 8/8 on the Q1–Q8 scorecard. The glycemic_index field is correctly set to null per the mandatory critical rule for negligible-carbohydrate protein foods — setting it to 0 or any numeric value would violate the execution prompt rule and cause a q4 gate failure. All other aspects are robust: core macros sourced from HPB Foodo F11010042 (exact match), saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central NDB 05011 with documentation, ontology anchors are honest closest_parent matches, and all three required health conditions are present with specific clinical guidance.
